sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a male, presents with a complaint of neck pain.
He reports experiencing pain since a car crash in February of this year, which he estimates to be the third car crash he has been in recently.
He describes the pain as sharp and incapacitating, rating it a 12 out of 10.
He reports difficulty moving his neck, stiffness, and pain on palpation on both the bony process and muscle.
He also reports experiencing headaches, dizziness, numbness in his left arm and right leg, and muscle spasms.
He denies visual disturbances and fatigue, but admits to feeling tired since the accident.
He mentions that his lawyer told him to come to this office and that he has been to a pain clinic and a therapist.
He is currently taking fentanyl, but has not received a prescription for several weeks.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure, pulse rate, oxygen saturation, and temperature were not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck pain is exacerbated by palpation on both the bony process and muscle.
He has a moderate range of motion in his neck, but pain on bending forward and backwards.
No bruising, swelling, or laceration were observed.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a neck sprain.
Problem list: Neck sprain; active.
Plan:
I prescribed Robaxin 1500mg orally every 6-8 hours for pain management.
I will refer the patient to physical therapy and we will obtain an MRI to further evaluate the extent of the injury.
I will also refer the patient to pain medication if the Robaxin does not provide adequate relief.
If the MRI results indicate that local injections are an option, we will explore this further.
Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with me after the MRI results are available.
If the patient's symptoms persist, we will re-evaluate the treatment plan.
The patient will be given a note excusing them from work until the MRI results are available.
